ex.u: اَگَرْ مَیں نے یَہ کِیَا ہوتَا تو ...
ex.r: agar ma͠i-ne ye kiyā hotā to
ex.e: If I had done this, then ...

اگربتی.e: اگر (agar, “agarwood”) + بتّی (battī, “candle”). Compare Punjabi ਅਗਰਬੱਤੀ (agarbattī)/اگربتی (agarbattī), Bengali আগরবাতি (agôrbati).

اگست.e: Borrowed from Portuguese agosto, influenced by English August.. Compare Konkani आगोस्त (āgosta), Marathi आगस्त (āgasta).

اگلا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀅𑀕𑁆𑀕𑀮 (aggala), from Sanskrit अग्र (ágra, “front; top”) + Middle Indo-Aryan -𑀮- (-la-).

senses.g: inflection of اَگْلا (aglā, “next”): — direct masculine plural
inflection of اَگْلا (aglā, “next”): — oblique masculine singular/plural

اگن.e: Semi-learned borrowing from Sanskrit अग्नि (agni, “fire”). Doublet of آگ (āg). Compare Bengali আগুন (agun).

اگنا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀉𑀕𑁆𑀕𑁂𑀇 (uggei), from Sanskrit *उद्गाति (*udgāti). Cognate with Gujarati ઉગવું (ugvũ), Punjabi اُگّنا (uggnā) / ਉੱਗਣਾ (uggaṇā), Sindhi اُڳڻ (ug̠ṇ) / उॻणु (ug̠aṇu).

اگنی.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit अग्नि (agní). Doublet of آگ (āg) and اَگَن (agan).
Learned borrowing from Sanskrit अग्नि (agní). Doublet of آگ (āg) and اَگَن (agan).

senses.g: except for what God has willed

الا ماشاء اللہ.e: Borrowed from Arabic إِلَّا مَا شَاءَ ٱللّٰهُ (ʔillā mā šāʔa llāhu, “Except, what God willed”).

الائچی.e: Ultimately from Proto-Dravidian *ēlakāy, probably via Sauraseni Prakrit. Related to Sanskrit एलीका (elīkā), Persian هل (hel), Hindi इलायची (ilāycī)

البتہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اَلْبَتَّه (albatta), from Arabic الْبَتَّةَ (al-battata).
Borrowed from Classical Persian اَلْبَتَّه (albatta), from Arabic الْبَتَّةَ (al-battata).

senses.g: the act of turning over, upside down, turned over

الٹنا.e: Inherited from Old Hindi उलटना (ulaṭanā), a verbal derivative from Prakrit उल्लट्ट (ullaṭṭa, “turned over”). Related to پلٹنا.

ex.u: چناچہ الجزائر میں اسلامک سالویشن فرنٹ کو اقتدار میں نہ آ نے دیا گیا اور ترکی میں رفاہ پارٹی کی بساط لپیٹ دی گئی۔
ex.e: Therefore, the Islamic Salvation Front was not allowed to come to power in Algeria and the Welfare Party in Turkey was overthrown.

الحاق.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian الحاق (ilhāq), from Arabic إِلْحَاق (ʔilḥāq).

الزام.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian الزام (ilzām, “requirement, commitment, tie”), from Arabic إِلْزَام (ʔilzām).

senses.g: name of the first letter of the Urdu alphabet; alif

الف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian الف (alif), from Arabic أَلِف (ʔalif).

الفاظ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اَلْفَاظ (alfāz), from Arabic أَلْفَاظ (ʔalfāẓ).

الکحل.e: Borrowed from English alcohol, with influence from Arabic الْكُحْل (al-kuḥl).

اللہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اَللّٰه (allāh), from Arabic اللّٰه (allāh). First attested as Old Hindi अलह (alaha).

اللہ اکبر.e: Borrowed from Arabic اللّٰهُ أَكْبَر (allāhu ʔakbar, “God is greater”)

اللہ حافظ.e: An Arabised rendition of خُدا حافِظ (xudā hāfiz), in which the Classical Persian خُدَا (xudā, “god”) is replaced by the Arabic اللّٰه (allāh, “God; Allah”). First attested in c. 19th century, but only came into common usage in Pakistan in the 1980s.

senses.g: a male given name from Chagatai

اللہ قلی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian and Chagatai اللهقلی (allāh-qulī, literally “slave of God”); compare Azerbaijani Allahqulu, Turkish Allahkulu.

الماری.e: Borrowed from Portuguese almario or armário, from Latin armarium (“closet, chest”), from arma (“weapons, tools”) + -ārium (“denoting a place where things are kept”).

المانیہ.e: Borrowed from Arabic أَلْمَانِيَا (ʔalmāniyā). Displaced by جَرْمَنی (jarmanī).

senses.g: an ornament of sound or sense

الو.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀉𑀮𑀽𑀅 (ulūa), from Sanskrit उलूक (úlūka). Cognate with Gujarati ઉલ્લૂ (ullū) and Punjabi الو (ullū).

الوداع.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اَلْوِدَاع (alwidā'), from Arabic الوَدَاع (al-wadāʕ). Doublet of وِداع (vidā').
Borrowed from Classical Persian اَلْوِدَاع (alwidā'), from Arabic الوَدَاع (al-wadāʕ). Doublet of وِداع (vidā').

senses.g: Allahabad (a city in Uttar Pradesh, India)

الہ آباد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اللهآباد (allāh-ābād), from Arabic اَللّٰه (allāh, “Allah”) + Classical Persian آباد (ābād, “city”).

الہام.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اِلْهَام (ilhām), borrowed from Arabic إِلْهَام (ʔilhām), from أَلْهَمَ (ʔalhama). First attested in c. 1657 as Middle Hindi الہام (alham /⁠ilhām⁠/).

senses.g: a male given name, Ilyas, from Arabic إِلْيَاس (ʔilyās)

الیاس.e: Borrowed from Arabic إِلْيَاس (ʔilyās), from Hebrew אליהו.

senses.g: a male given name from Arabic

الیسع.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian الیسع (alyasa’), from Arabic الْيَسَع (al-yasaʕ).

اما.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اما (ammā), from Arabic أَمَّا (ʔammā).
Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀅𑀁𑀫𑀸 (aṃmā), from Sanskrit अम्बा (ambā).

senses.g: the Emirates, short for the United Arab Emirates
possession of command; the office, and authority of a commander, governor, etc. (amīr)
district under the authority of a governor
command, authority, power, dominion, rule, sovereignty
dignity, grandeur, state, magnificence, pomp, display

امارت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اِمَارَت (imārat), borrowed from Arabic إِمَارَة (ʔimāra), from أَمَرَ (ʔamara).

امام.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اِمَام (imām), borrowed from Arabic إِمَام (ʔimām).

senses.g: a shrine dedicated to Imam Hussain
an office or jurisdiction of an imam.

امامت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اِمَامَت (imāmat), borrowed from Arabic إِمَامة (ʔimāma), from إِمَام (ʔimām) + ـَة (-a).

senses.g: trust; deposit or possession entrusted to someone for safekeeping.
a trust (fund setup for a beneficiary)
trusteeship; the office / department of an amin – trustee.
divine faith as revealed by God to man with which one can attain His abode

امانت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اَمَانَت (amānat), borrowed from Arabic أَمَانَة (ʔamāna), from أَمُنَ (ʔamuna).

senses.g: "imported government disapproved," or "imported government not allowed," a phrase used by Pakistan Tehreek-e-Insaf and Imran Khan during their rallies.

امت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اُمَّت (ummat), borrowed from Arabic أُمَّة (ʔumma).

امتحان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اِمْتِحَان (imtihān), borrowed from Arabic اِمْتِحَان (imtiḥān), from اِمْتَحَنَ (imtaḥana). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi امتحان (amtḥan /⁠imtihān⁠/).

senses.g: oblique plural of اِمْتِحان (imtihān, “test; exam; trial”)
related to anything of the ummah

امتی.e: Borrowed from Arabic أُمَّتِي (ʔummatī, “my people”), from أُمَّة (ʔumma) + ـِي (-ī).

امر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اَمْر (amr), from Arabic أَمْر (ʔamr).

امرت.e: Borrowed from Sanskrit अमृत (amṛta). First attested in c. 1611 as Middle Hindi امرت (amrt).

امرتسر.e: Borrowed from Punjabi ਅੰਮ੍ਰਿਤਸਰ (ammritsar), from Sanskrit अमृतसरस् (amṛtasaras).

senses.g: America, the Americas (a supercontinent consisting of North America, Central America and South America regarded as a whole; in full, the Americas)
America, United States of America (a country in North America; in full, United States of America; official name: ریاستہائے متحدہ (riyāsat’hā-e-muttahida))

امریکا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian امریکا, from Arabic أَمْرِيكَا (ʔamrīkā).

senses.g: alternative spelling of اَمْرِیکا (amrīkā): — America, United States of America (a country in North America; in full, United States of America)
alternative spelling of اَمْرِیکا (amrīkā): — America, the Americas (a supercontinent consisting of North America, Central America and South America regarded as a whole; in full, the Americas)

امکان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اِمْکَان (imkān), borrowed from Arabic إِمْكَان (ʔimkān), from أَمْكَنَ (ʔamkana). First attested in 1708 as Middle Hindi امکان (amkan /⁠imkān⁠/).

امل.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian امل (amal), from Arabic أَمَل (ʔamal).

املا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اِمْلَا (imlā), from Arabic إِمْلَاء (ʔimlāʔ).

املی.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit *𑀇𑀫𑁆𑀮𑀺𑀕𑀸 (*imligā), from Sanskrit अम्लिका (amlikā). Cognate with Bhojpuri इमली (imᵊlī), Gujarati આમલી (āmalī), Punjabi اِمْلی (īmlī) / ਇਮਲੀ (īmlī).

senses.g: synonym of امیتابھ بدھ (amitābh buddh, “Amitābha Buddha”)
Amitābha Buddha, the principal deity of Pure Land Buddhism.

امید.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian امید (umēd, umīd, ummēd, ummīd).

senses.g: a male given name, Ummid, from Persian

امید.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian امید (umēd, umīd, ummēd, ummīd).

senses.g: candidate, contestant (in an election or examination etc.)

امیدوار.e: First attested in c. 1609 as Middle Hindi امیدوار (ummēdvār), borrowed from Classical Persian اُمّیدْوَار (ummēdwār). By surface analysis, اُمِّید (ummīd, “hope”) + وَار (vār).
First attested in c. 1609 as Middle Hindi امیدوار (ummēdvār), borrowed from Classical Persian اُمّیدْوَار (ummēdwār). By surface analysis, اُمِّید (ummīd, “hope”) + وَار (vār).

امیر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian امیر (amīr), from Arabic أَمِير (ʔamīr).
Borrowed from Classical Persian امیر (amīr), from Arabic أَمِير (ʔamīr).

senses.g: a female given name, Amina or Ameena, from Arabic, masculine equivalent امین (amīn)

امینہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اَمِینَه (amīna), borrowed from Arabic أَمِينَة (ʔamīna), from أَمِين (ʔamīn) + ـَة (-a).

senses.g: A negating prefix. non-, un-, a-, an-, etc.

ان.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀅𑀡- (aṇa-), from Sanskrit अन्- (an-), from Proto-Indo-European *n̥- (“not, un-”).

senses.g: third-person plural objective personal pronoun: them
third-person singular objective personal pronoun: him, (oblique case) her
third-person plural objective personal pronoun: them
third-person singular objective personal pronoun: him, (oblique case) her

ـان.e: Inherited from Sanskrit -इनी (-inī), feminine of -इन् (-in, possessive suffix).

ex.u: کچھ ان پڑھ لوگ پھر بھی ذہین ہوتے ہیں۔
ex.r: kućh an paṛh log zahīn hote hẽ.

ان شاء اللہ.e: Learned borrowing from Arabic إِنْ شَاءَ ٱللّٰهُ (ʔin šāʔa llāhu).

senses.g: to be ruined, destroyed; damaged beyond repair

ex.u: بیچارہ کیلکیولیٹر تو انا للہ ہو گیا۔
ex.r: becāra kailkyūleṭar to innā lillāhi ho gayā.

انا للہ ہونا.e: From اِنَّا لِلّٰہِ (innā lillāhi, ellipsis of اِنَّا لِلّٰہِ وَ اِنَّا اِلَیہِ رَاجِعُون (innā lillāhi va innā ilaihi rāji'ūn, “inna Lillahi wa inna ilayhi raji'un”)) + ہونَا (honā).

ex.u: آج کل انار مہنگے ہو گئے ہیں
ex.r: āj kal anār mahaṅge ho gae ha͠i.
ex.e: These days, pomegranates have become costly.

senses.g: Anarkali (a legendary courtesan from the Mughal era)
Anarkali Bazaar (a neighbourhood of Lahore, Punjab, Pakistan)

انارکلی بازار.e: Named after Anarkali, from اَنارکَلِی (anārkalī) + بازار (bāzār).

انبیاء.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اَنْبِیَاء (anbiyā'), from Arabic أَنْبِيَاء (ʔanbiyāʔ), plural of نَبِيّ (nabiyy).

انتباہ.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian انتباه (intibāh), from Arabic اِنْتِبَاه (intibāh).

انتخاب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اِنْتِخَاب, from Arabic اِنْتِخَاب (intiḵāb).

انتخابات.e: Borrowed from Arabic اِنْتِخَابَات (intiḵābāt), plural of Arabic اِنْتِخَاب (intiḵāb).

ex.u: آج سینیٹ میں اَنْتْرِم وَزِیرِ اَعْظَم کی چُنَاؤ ہوگی۔
ex.r: āj seneṭ mẽ antrim vazīr-i a'zam kī cunāo hogī.
ex.e: Today an interim prime minister will be chosen by the Senate.

انتظار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اِنْتِظَار (intizār), borrowed from Arabic اِنْتِظَار (intiẓār), from اِنْتَظَرَ (intaẓara).

انتظام.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اِنْتِظَام (intizām), from Arabic اِنْتِظَام (intiẓām, “order, regularity”). First attested in c. 1678 as Middle Hindi انتظام (antz̤am /⁠intizām⁠/).

انتظامی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian انتظامی (intizāmī). By surface analysis, انتظام (intizām, “arrangement, administration”) + ی (ī).

انتظامیہ.e: Ultimately borrowed from the Arabic ن ظ م (n ẓ m) root.

انتقال.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اِنْتِقَال (intiqāl), from Arabic اِنْتِقَال (intiqāl).

انتقام.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اِنْتِقَام (intiqām), from Arabic اِنْتِقَام (intiqām), verbal noun of اِنْتَقَمَ (intaqama), from the root ن ق م (n q m).

senses.g: extreme (ie. the extreme end of a point, scale)

انتہا.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اِنْتِهَا (intihā), borrowed from Arabic اِنْتِهَاء (intihāʔ), from اِنْتَهَى (intahā). First attested in c. 1582 as Middle Hindi انتہا (antha /⁠intihā⁠/).

senses.g: Antarctica (the southernmost continent, south of the Southern Ocean, containing the South Pole)

ex.u: اس کا انجام ٹھیک نہیں ہو گا۔
ex.r: is kā añjām ṭhīk nahī̃ ho gā
ex.e: The result of it won't be right.

انجام.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian انجام (anjām), from Middle Persian [script needed] (hanǰām, “end, completion, outcome”).

ex.u: کسی انجانی سچائی کے لیے یہ جنگ لڑا گیا ہے۔
ex.r: kisī anjānī sacāī ke liye ye jang laṛā gayā hai.
ex.e: This war has been fought for some unknown truth.

انجماد.e: Borrowed from Arabic اِنْجِماد (injimād), from Arabic جُمُود (jumūd).

ex.u: اس انجمن میں آپ کو آنا ہے بار بار
ex.r: is anjuman mẽ āp ko ānā hai bār bār
ex.e: You will have to come again and again to this gathering

انجمن.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian انجمن (anjuman, “meeting, assembly”). First attested in c. 1564 as Middle Hindi انجمن (anjmn /⁠anjuman⁠/).

اندر.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اندر (andar, “inside, within”).
Inherited from Sanskrit उन्दुर (undura), उन्दुरु (unduru), उन्दरु (undaru), etc. from a lost Vedic substrate. Compare Bengali ইন্দুর (indur), ইঁদুর (ĩdur).

senses.g: Indra (king of the deities and god of rain and thunder)
a male given name, Indar, Inder, or Indra, from Sanskrit, of Sikh and Hindu usage

اندر.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit इन्द्र (indra). Compare Punjabi اندر (iṇdar), Kalasha اِن (in), Kamkata-viri ایںدر (ī˜drʻ), and Prasuni اِندر (indr).

senses.g: a walled city (an area of a city or town which is surrounded by gates or doors, usually a historical area)
Andalusia (a historical region and autonomous community in southern Spain, the most populated and second largest of the seventeen autonomous communities that constitute Spain)

اندلس.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اندلس (andalus, andulus), borrowed from Arabic الأندلس (al-ʔandalus, al-ʔandulus).

senses.g: inflection of اَنْدیشَہ (andeśa, “thought; consideration”): — direct plural
inflection of اَنْدیشَہ (andeśa, “thought; consideration”): — oblique/vocative singular

اندیکھا.e: Compounded from ان- (an-) + past participle of دیکھنا (dekhnā).

اندھا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀅𑀁𑀥 (aṃdha), from Sanskrit अन्ध (andha).
Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀅𑀁𑀥 (aṃdha), from Sanskrit अन्ध (andha).

اندھکار.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit अन्धकार (andhakāra). Doublet of اَنْدھیرا (andherā).

اندھیرا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀅𑀁𑀥𑀬𑀸𑀭 (aṃdhayāra), from Sanskrit अन्धकार (andhakāra).
Inherited from Prakrit 𑀅𑀁𑀥𑀬𑀸𑀭 (aṃdhayāra), from Sanskrit अन्धकार (andhakāra).

senses.g: inflection of اَنْدھا (andhā): — direct masculine plural
inflection of اَنْدھا (andhā): — oblique/vocative masculine singular/plural

انڈا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀅𑀁𑀟 (aṃḍa), from Sanskrit अण्ड (aṇḍá), आण्ड (āṇḍá), or अण्डक (aṇḍaka). Compare Punjabi آنڈا (ānḍā) / ਆਂਡਾ (āṇḍā), Sindhi آنو (āno) / आनो, Nepali अण्डा (aṇḍā), Gujarati ઈંડું (ī̃ḍũ), Marathi अंडे (aṇḍe), Konkani अंडें (aṇḍẽ), Bengali আণ্ডা (anḍa), এণ্ডা (enḍa).

انڈکوش.e: Learned borrowing from Classical Sanskrit अण्डकोष (aṇḍakoṣa).

senses.g: Indonesia (a country in the Southeast Asia, Asia)

انڈیا.e: Borrowed from English India. Doublet of ہِنْدِیَہ (hindiya).

انسان.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اِنْسَان (insān), borrowed from Arabic إِنْسَان (ʔinsān), from Proto-Semitic *ʔināš- (“man, person”).

انسانی.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian انسانی (insānī), from Arabic إِنْسَانِيّ (ʔinsāniyy).

انسانیت.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اِنْسَانِیَّت (insāniyyat), from Arabic إِنْسَانِيَّة (ʔinsāniyya).

انسداد.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اِنْسِداد, from Arabic اِنْسِدَاد (insidād).

انصاف.e: Borrowed from Arabic إِنْصَاف (ʔinṣāf), from أَنْصَفَ (ʔanṣafa).

انطاکیہ.e: Borrowed from Arabic أَنْطَاكِيَة (ʔanṭākiya), from Ancient Greek Ἀντιόχεια (Antiókheia).

senses.g: Antilyas (a city in Mount Lebanon Governorate, Lebanon)

انعام.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اِنْعَام (in'ām, “reward, bonus, gratuity”), borrowed from Arabic إِنْعَام (ʔinʕām), from أَنْعَمَ (ʔanʕama).

انقلاب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اِنْقِلَاب (inqilāb), from Arabic اِنْقِلَاب (inqilāb).

انکار.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اِنْکَار (inkār), borrowed from Arabic إِنْكَار (ʔinkār), from أَنْكَرَ (ʔankara).

انکشاف.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اِنْکِشَاف (inkišāf), from Arabic اِنْكِشَاف (inkišāf).

senses.g: respectful term of address for any elder man

انگریز.e: Borrowed from Portuguese inglês, from Old French angleis, from Old English Englisċ.

senses.g: English; of or relating to English people

انگریز.e: Borrowed from Portuguese inglês, from Old French angleis, from Old English Englisċ.

senses.g: English; of or relating to the English language or English people

انگریزی.e: From اَن٘گْریز (aṅgrez, “English or British people”) + ـی (-ī)
From اَن٘گْریز (aṅgrez, “English or British people”) + ـی (-ī)

انگڑائی.e: From اَن٘گْڑانا (aṅgṛānā) + ـَائی (-āī), ultimately derived from Sanskrit अङ्ग (aṅga, “limb”).

senses.g: England, Inglistan (a constituent country of the United Kingdom)
England (a constituent country of the United Kingdom)

انگوٹھا.e: Inherited from Sanskrit अङ्गुष्ठ (aṅguṣṭha). Cognate with Gujarati અંગૂઠો (aṅgūṭho), Marathi अंगठा (aṅgṭhā), and Marwari अंगूठौ (aṅgūṭhau).

senses.g: colored of green grapes; light lime (color)
Angola (a country in the Central Africa, Africa)

انگیا.e: From Sanskrit अङ्गिका (aṅgikā). Cognate with Gujarati અંગિયો (aṅgiyo) and Marwari अंगिया (ãṅgiyā).

انل.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit अनल m (anala, “fire”).
Learned borrowing from Sanskrit अनिल m (anila, “air; wind; god of the wind”).

senses.g: god of the winds, a form of Vayu

انل.e: Learned borrowing from Sanskrit अनिल m (anila, “air; wind; god of the wind”).

senses.g: interpretation (of something said), repeating someone's words

انوکھا.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀅𑀡𑁄𑀓𑁆𑀔 (aṇokkha), *𑀅𑀡𑀯𑁂𑀓𑁆𑀔 (*aṇavekkha), from Sanskrit अनपेक्ष (anapekṣa). Compare Punjabi اَݨوکھا (aṇokhā) / ਅਣੋਖਾ (aṇokhā) and Saraiki اَنوکْھڑا (anokhṛā).

senses.g: inflection of اَنوکھا (anokhā, “unique, strange”): — direct masculine plural
inflection of اَنوکھا (anokhā, “unique, strange”): — oblique masculine singular/plural
alternative spelling of اِنْہوں نے (inhõ ne)

انھوں نے.e: See the etymology of the corresponding lemma form.

senses.g: alternative spelling of اُنْہوں نے (unhõ ne)

انھوں نے.e: See the etymology of the corresponding lemma form.

senses.g: third-person singular subjective personal pronoun of unspecified gender: they
the one upstairs; God, the Supreme Being

اوپر والا.e: اوپر (ūpar, “up, above”) + ـوالا (-vālā, “one”), literally “the one above”.

senses.g: Uthal (a city in Lasbela district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

اودا.e: Inherited from Prakrit 𑀉𑀤𑁆𑀤𑀸𑀬𑀇 (uddāyaï, “is polished, is very clear”), from an unattested Sanskrit *उद्दात (*uddāta, “cleansed, bright, clear”), from उद्- (ud-, “over, above”) + दात (dāta, “cleansed, purified”), the latter from the root दै (dai, “to purify, cleanse”).

Inherited from Prakrit 𑀉𑀤𑁆𑀤𑀸𑀬𑀇 (uddāyaï, “is polished, is very clear”), from an unattested Sanskrit *उद्दात (*uddāta, “cleansed, bright, clear”), from उद्- (ud-, “over, above”) + दात (dāta, “cleansed, purified”), the latter from the root दै (dai, “to purify, cleanse”).

senses.g: Awadh (a historical region of Uttar Pradesh, India)

اوڈیشہ.e: Borrowed from Odia ଓଡ଼ିଶା (oṛiśā), in turn derived from Sanskrit ओड्रदेश (oḍradeśa, literally “land of the Odras”), a compound of ओड्र (oḍra, “Odra”) + देश (deśa, “land, country”). Doublet of اُڑِیسَہ (uṛīsa) and اُڑِیسَا (uṛīsā).

اور.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀅𑀯𑀭 (avara), from Sanskrit अपर (ápara, “other, different”). Cognate with Punjabi ہوْر (hor).
Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀅𑀯𑀭 (avara), from Sanskrit अपर (ápara, “other, different”). Cognate with Punjabi ہوْر (hor).
Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀅𑀯𑀭 (avara), from Sanskrit अपर (ápara, “other, different”). Cognate with Punjabi ہوْر (hor).

senses.g: Ormara (a city in Gwadar district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

اورماڑا.e: Named after General Ormoz of Alexander the Great's army.

senses.g: a male given name, Aurangzeb, from Persian

اورنگزیب.e: Borrowed from Classical Persian اَورَنْگزیب (awrang-zēb, literally “Ornament of the Throne”).

senses.g: Uri (a town, a tehsil in Baramulla district, Jammu and Kashmir, India)

اوس.e: Inherited from Sauraseni Prakrit 𑀑𑀲𑀸 (osā), from Sanskrit अवश्या (avaśyā). Cognate with Bengali ওস (ōs).

senses.g: Osaka (the capital city of Osaka Prefecture, Japan)
Osaka (a prefecture on the island of Honshū, Japan)

اوساکا.e: Borrowed from English Osaka, from Japanese 大(おお)阪(さか) (Ōsaka, literally “large hill”).

senses.g: Usta Muhammad (a city in Usta Muhammad district, Balochistan, Pakistan)

اوقات.e: Borrowed from Arabic أَوْقَات (ʔawqāt), the plural of وَقْت (waqt), whence Urdu وَقْت (vaqt, “time”). Although this is the formal plural of vaqt, it is generally used as a singular feminine noun with a different sense from "time".

اوقیانوس.e: Borrowed from Arabic أُوقْيَانُوس (ʔūqyānūs), from Ancient Greek Ὠκεανός (Ōkeanós). Doublet of قامُوس (qāmūs).
